---
title: "Project Overview"
---

Recent research suggests that demand in data science and business analytics is not being driven only by highly specialized technical skills. A major theme across the literature is that employers want people who can work with data and also connect that work to business decisions, strategy, and organizational goals. Instead of viewing analytics as just a technical function, these studies show that it is increasingly tied to communication, problem-solving, and adaptability in the workplace [@dickson2023integrated; @mahmud2024ai].

Studies based on job postings especially point to the growing importance of analyst-focused and business-facing roles. In their review of 786 job postings, Booker et al. found that soft skills made up a large share of the most frequently requested qualifications, with communication and teamwork appearing often alongside technical skills such as visualization, data cleaning, data extraction, and programming [@booker2024data]. Kurpiela and Teuteberg also found that business analytics is changing strategic job profiles, with many competency patterns overlapping with roles like business intelligence analyst, project manager, and data analyst [@kurpiela2023changing]. Taken together, these studies suggest that employers are looking for people who can not only handle data, but also use it to support decisions and broader organizational change.

Other research expands this discussion by looking at how education and workforce planning should respond to these shifts. Dickson and Zlatev argue that data and business analytics education should be more closely aligned with labor market expectations so that students build skills that match real employer demand [@dickson2023integrated]. In a similar way, Mahmud et al. explain that AI-powered workforce analytics can help identify skill gaps and forecast future labor needs, which reinforces the growing value of workers who can operate in data-heavy and AI-supported environments [@mahmud2024ai].

At the same time, this body of research has some limitations. Much of it relies on job postings or broader workforce frameworks, which are useful for identifying hiring trends but do not always show how those skills are used after people are hired. It also does not provide one single cross-industry snapshot of 2024 job demand, so it is difficult to rank roles with complete certainty. Even so, the overall pattern is pretty clear: employers seem to value a mix of technical and interpersonal skills, and demand appears strongest for roles that combine analytics with business understanding and communication rather than for narrowly focused advanced AI positions alone [@booker2024data; @kurpiela2023changing].
